content.problemStatement:
The Chef is planning a buffet for the DirectiPlex inauguration party, and everyone is invited.  On their way in, each guest picks up a sheet of paper containing a random number (this number may be repeated).  The guests then sit down on a round table with their friends.   

 The Chef now decides that he would like to play a game. He asks you to pick a random person from your table and have them read their number out loud.  Then, moving clockwise around the table, each person will read out their number.  The goal is to find that set of numbers which forms an increasing subsequence. All people owning these numbers will be eligible for a lucky draw! One of the software developers is very excited about this prospect, and wants to maximize the number of people who are eligible for the lucky draw. So, he decides to write a program that decides who should read their number first so as to maximize the number of people that are eligible for the lucky draw. Can you beat him to it?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10000) – the number of guests. The second line contains N integers a1 a2 … aN (0 ≤ ai ≤ 10^6) – the numbers written on the sheets in clockwise order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the maximum possible length of a strictly increasing subsequence after choosing an optimal starting guest.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Case 1: All numbers are equal, any rotation gives LIS = 1.
Case 2: Rotating to start with 1 yields sequence 1 3 2, LIS = 2 (1,2).
Case 3: Starting from the fourth guest gives order 1 5 2 4 8 6, LIS = 4 (1,2,4,6).
Only one guest exists, so the longest increasing subsequence has length 1.
The original order is already strictly increasing, giving LIS = 4. Any other rotation reduces the LIS, but the maximum remains 4.

hidden.label: general case 2 – almost sorted after rotation

solution.approach: For a fixed starting position we obtain a linear order of the N numbers by rotating the original circular array. The length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence (LIS) of this order can be computed in O(N log N) using the classic patience‑sorting method (maintain a tail array and binary‑search the position of each element). We repeat this procedure for every possible start (0 … N‑1) and keep the maximum LIS length found. The overall complexity is O(N^2 log N) which easily fits the limits (N ≤ 10000, T ≤ 15).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 log N) per test case (N rotations, each LIS in O(N log N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for the tail array used in the LIS routine.
